source_paper_title: Congruency-Constrained TU Problems Beyond the Bimodular Case
source_paper_bibtex: @article{N_gele_2024, title={Congruency-Constrained TU Problems Beyond the Bimodular Case}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1381}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1381}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Nägele, Martin and Santiago, Richard and Zenklusen, Rico}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1303–1348} }
context:
Page 2, Section 1 (Introduction)

In particular, there is a long-standing open question on whether ILPs are efficiently solvable if their constraint matrix is \(\Delta\)-modular for constant \(\Delta\).
title: Polynomial-time solvability of integer programs with bounded subdeterminants setting
background_latex:
An integer linear program (ILP) is
\[\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\},\]
where \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\), \(b\in\mathbb Z^k\), and \(c\in\mathbb Z^n\). For \(\Delta\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), a matrix \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\) is called \(\Delta\)-modular if:
1) \(A\) has full column rank \(n\); and
2) every \(n\times n\) submatrix \(B\) of \(A\) satisfies \(|\det(B)|\le \Delta\).
Equivalently, the maximum absolute value of an \(n\times n\) subdeterminant of \(A\) is at most \(\Delta\).

A special and classical case is \(\Delta=1\), where \(A\) is totally unimodular (TU): every square submatrix has determinant in \(\{-1,0,1\}\). In that case, linear optimization over \(\{x\in\mathbb R^n: Ax\le b\}\) yields an integral optimal solution whenever \(b\) is integral.

The paper studies an important subclass of bounded-subdeterminant ILPs: congruency-constrained TU problems, i.e., TU systems plus one congruency constraint \(\gamma^\top x\equiv r\pmod m\). It proves feasibility can be decided in strongly polynomial randomized time for modulus \(m=3\), and more generally for prime \(m\) when all but at most two residues are allowed.

Despite this progress, the broad question for general \(\Delta\)-modular matrices remains unresolved.
problem_definition_latex:
Design an algorithm that, for every fixed constant \(\Delta\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), solves in time polynomial in the encoding length of \((A,b,c)\) the ILP
\[\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\},\]
under the promise that \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\) is \(\Delta\)-modular (i.e., has full column rank and all \(n\times n\) subdeterminants have absolute value at most \(\Delta\)).
latex:
An integer linear program (ILP) is
\[\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\},\]
where \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\), \(b\in\mathbb Z^k\), and \(c\in\mathbb Z^n\). For \(\Delta\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), a matrix \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\) is called \(\Delta\)-modular if:
1) \(A\) has full column rank \(n\); and
2) every \(n\times n\) submatrix \(B\) of \(A\) satisfies \(|\det(B)|\le \Delta\).
Equivalently, the maximum absolute value of an \(n\times n\) subdeterminant of \(A\) is at most \(\Delta\).

A special and classical case is \(\Delta=1\), where \(A\) is totally unimodular (TU): every square submatrix has determinant in \(\{-1,0,1\}\). In that case, linear optimization over \(\{x\in\mathbb R^n: Ax\le b\}\) yields an integral optimal solution whenever \(b\) is integral.

The paper studies an important subclass of bounded-subdeterminant ILPs: congruency-constrained TU problems, i.e., TU systems plus one congruency constraint \(\gamma^\top x\equiv r\pmod m\). It proves feasibility can be decided in strongly polynomial randomized time for modulus \(m=3\), and more generally for prime \(m\) when all but at most two residues are allowed.

Despite this progress, the broad question for general \(\Delta\)-modular matrices remains unresolved.

Design an algorithm that, for every fixed constant \(\Delta\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), solves in time polynomial in the encoding length of \((A,b,c)\) the ILP
\[\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\},\]
under the promise that \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\) is \(\Delta\)-modular (i.e., has full column rank and all \(n\times n\) subdeterminants have absolute value at most \(\Delta\)).
open_problem_quote: In particular, there is a long-standing open question on whether ILPs are efficiently solvable if their constraint matrix is \(\Delta\)-modular for constant \(\Delta\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement asks for an efficient algorithm for all instances with a given bounded-subdeterminant parameter, so a single finite counterexample instance is not meaningful unless one targets a specific proposed algorithm or complexity hypothesis. Automated search could generate hard instances, but there is no crisp, efficiently checkable property whose violation would refute the existence of some (unknown) polynomial-time algorithm.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate algorithms or reductions for restricted subclasses of \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs and score them by empirical success on generated instances and runtime proxies, potentially discovering useful heuristics or structural decompositions. However, converting such discoveries into a general polynomial-time algorithm (or hardness proof) requires substantial theoretical insight beyond what a computable fitness signal can reliably guide.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Nägele–Santiago–Zenklusen) frames the fixed-\(\Delta\) bounded-subdeterminant ILP question as a major open frontier beyond total unimodularity, and develops new machinery in the congruency-constrained TU setting (CCTU/CCTUF and the relaxed-residue variant R-CCTUF). Its decomposition lemma for TU systems, together with flatness and proximity bounds of size \(m-|R|\), enables a strongly polynomial randomized feasibility algorithm for modulus \(m=3\), and more generally for prime \(m\) when all but at most two residues are allowed. This highlights a plausible route to bounded-minor ILPs via reductions to TU plus modular constraints, but such reductions are only known for \(\Delta=2\) (bimodular) and remain unavailable for general \(\Delta>2\).

Forward-citing work has expanded the tractable landscape mainly by adding structural assumptions. The strongest algorithmic progress toward the open problem comes from results on totally \(\Delta\)-modular matrices that are ‘nearly TU’ in matroidal/graphical senses: the cographic nearly-network case admits strongly polynomial optimization, and related work handles matrices that are two-nonzeros-per-row except for a constant-size perturbation. In parallel, a separate line advances the strictly \(\Delta\)-modular regime (full-size minors in \(\{0,\pm\Delta\}\)) via reductions to group-constrained TU feasibility and propagation through Seymour decomposition, yielding strongly polynomial randomized feasibility for \(\Delta\le 4\) (including the first composite case \(\Delta=4\)). Extended-formulation constructions further give polynomial-time optimization for strictly \(\Delta\)-modular cographic translated cones. These results collectively suggest that combining TU decomposition with modular/group constraints is powerful, but current techniques still require either strict minor structure, cographic/network structure, or cone/translation assumptions.

On the tools side, improved proximity and flatness bounds in terms of \(\Delta_{n-1}(A)\) and \(\Delta_n(A)\) sharpen the geometric control needed for enumeration or augmentation algorithms, and become dimension-independent in certain restricted minor-value regimes (including some strictly \(\Delta\)-modular factorizations \(A=TB\)). However, for general \(\Delta\)-modular matrices these bounds still typically scale with dimension, and no known method turns them into a polynomial-time (or strongly polynomial) algorithm for optimization. Overall, the general fixed-\(\Delta\) \(\Delta\)-modular ILP solvability problem remains open; progress continues through (i) identifying reductions from bounded-minor ILPs to TU plus a bounded number of modular/extra constraints, and (ii) extending group-constrained TU methods from feasibility to optimization and from strictly \(\Delta\)-modular to general \(\Delta\)-modular matrices.

sources.title: Integer programs with nearly totally unimodular matrices: the cographic case
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves (Theorem 1) that for every fixed constant \(\Delta\), the integer program \(\max\{p^\top x: Mx\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\}\) can be solved in strongly polynomial time for the class of totally \(\Delta\)-modular matrices \(M\) that become the transpose of a network matrix after deleting a constant number of rows and columns. Equivalently, after reductions it solves Problem 1/3: optimize over a TU system \(Ax\le b\) plus \(k\) extra constraints \(Wx\le d\) (or \(Wx=d\)) with \(k\) constant, under the promise that \([A;W]\) is totally \(\Delta\)-modular, in the cographic (transpose-network) nearly-TU case.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm for ILPs with \(\Delta\)-modular constraint matrix \(A\) (full column rank; all \(n\times n\) subdeterminants bounded by \(\Delta\)), for each fixed \(\Delta\), with no further structural assumptions. This paper does not resolve that general question. Instead it gives a strong positive result for a substantial subclass: totally \(\Delta\)-modular matrices that are ‘nearly TU’ in a specific sense—after deleting \(O(1)\) rows/columns one obtains the transpose of a network matrix (cographic regular-matroid building block). It develops (i) a proximity/augmentation theorem for TU+\(O(1)\) extra constraints and (ii) a graph-minor-based decomposition excluding rooted \(K_{2,t}\) minors to enable dynamic programming. Thus it provides partial progress/evidence toward the general bounded-subdeterminant ILP conjecture, but leaves the full \(\Delta\)-modular case open.

sources.title: Advances on strictly Δ -modular IPs
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves that strictly \(\Delta\)-modular IP feasibility is decidable in strongly polynomial randomized time for \(\Delta\le 4\). Concretely, it establishes: there exists a strongly polynomial-time randomized algorithm that, given a strictly 4-modular IP \(\{x\in\mathbb Z^n: Ax\le b\}\), either finds a feasible integer solution or certifies infeasibility (Theorem 4). Equivalently, it gives a strongly polynomial randomized algorithm for group-constrained TU feasibility (GCTUF) when the finite abelian group \(G\) has \(|G|\le 4\) (Theorem 5), via a strongly polynomial reduction from strictly \(\Delta\)-modular feasibility to multi-congruency-constrained TU feasibility with \(\prod_i m_i=\Delta\) (Lemma 8).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm (for each fixed constant \(\Delta\)) to solve general \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs (optimization) with full-rank \(A\) and all \(n\times n\) subdeterminants bounded by \(\Delta\). This paper does not resolve that conjecture. Instead it advances a related, weaker conjecture (Conjecture 2) for the subclass of strictly \(\Delta\)-modular matrices (all \(n\times n\) minors in \(\{-\Delta,0,\Delta\}\)) and only for the feasibility problem, not optimization. It extends prior prime-\(\Delta\) techniques by introducing group-constrained TU feasibility and proving feasibility for strictly \(\Delta\)-modular IPs when \(\Delta\le 4\), including the first non-prime case \(\Delta=4\). These techniques (reductions to MCCTU/GCTUF, Seymour decomposition, pattern/averaging arguments) are relevant tools toward the broader \(\Delta\)-modular conjecture, but they do not cover general \(\Delta\)-modular matrices nor provide an optimization algorithm.

sources.title: Totally -Modular IPs with Two Non-zeros in Most Rows
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives a strongly polynomial-time algorithm for a structured subclass of totally \(\Delta\)-modular integer programs. Concretely (Theorem 4), for fixed constants \(\Delta,k\), it solves IPs with constraint matrix \(M=\begin{bmatrix}A & B\\ W & D\end{bmatrix}\) that is totally \(\Delta\)-modular, where after removing at most \(k\) rows and \(k\) columns (the \(W,D,B\) blocks), the remaining block \(A\) has at most two nonzeros per row. Equivalently, it solves Problem 2 (and via reduction, Problem 3):
\[\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\; Wx=d,\; \ell\le x\le u,\; x\in\mathbb Z^n\}\]
when \(\begin{bmatrix}A\\ W\end{bmatrix}\) is totally \(\Delta\)-modular, \(A\in\{0,\pm1\}^{m\times n}\) has exactly two nonzeros per row, and \(k\) is constant.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm for general \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs (bounded \(n\times n\) subdeterminants, full column rank) for each fixed \(\Delta\). This paper does not solve that general case; instead it proves polynomial/strongly-polynomial solvability under an additional strong sparsity/structure condition: all but a constant number of rows/columns have at most two nonzeros per row (graph-incidence structure), plus a constant number \(k\) of extra constraints/variables. This is genuine progress toward the conjecture for bounded-subdeterminant IPs, extending Fiorini et al. (two nonzeros per row) to allow a constant number of additional “unifying” constraints/variables, but it leaves the unrestricted \(\Delta\)-modular case open.

sources.title: Extended formulations for the integer hull of strictly Δ-modular cographic polyhedral cones
sources.theorem_or_result: Main result (Theorem 1): For fixed \(\Delta\in\mathbb N\), if \(A\in\mathbb Z^{m\times n}\) has rank \(n\), is strictly \(\Delta\)-modular (every \(n\times n\) subdeterminant is in \(\{0,\pm\Delta\}\)), the row matroid \(M(A^\top)\) is cographic, and \(b\in\mathrm{span}(A)\) (so \(P(A,b)=\{x:Ax\le b\}\) is a translated cone), then the integer hull \(\mathrm{conv}(P(A,b)\cap\mathbb Z^n)\) admits a polynomial-size extended formulation of size \(O(n^{\Delta})\) constructible in polynomial time. Consequently, feasibility/optimization over \(P(A,b)\cap\mathbb Z^n\) is solvable in polynomial time for this class via LP in the extension.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm for ILPs with \(\Delta\)-modular constraint matrix \(A\) for every fixed \(\Delta\), with no additional structure assumptions. This paper does not resolve that conjecture, but proves polynomial-time solvability (via constructible compact extended formulations) for a structured subclass: strictly \(\Delta\)-modular matrices whose row matroid is cographic and with right-hand side \(b\) in \(\mathrm{span}(A)\) (translated cones). The proof reduces the integer hull (in slack space) to a congruency-constrained circulation problem and gives a generalized extended formulation for circulations with congruency constraints (Lemma 6). The paper also shows conditions (cographic matroid and \(b\in\mathrm{span}(A)\)) are necessary for their EF approach by citing counterexamples where compact EFs fail even for strictly bimodular matrices.

sources.title: Advances on strictly -modular IPs
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves feasibility of strictly \(\Delta\)-modular IPs for \(\Delta\le 4\) can be decided in strongly polynomial randomized time. Concretely: Theorem 4 states there is a strongly polynomial-time randomized algorithm that either finds \(x\in\mathbb Z^n\) with \(Ax\le b\) for a strictly 4-modular matrix \(A\) (i.e., all \(n\times n\) minors in \(\{-4,0,4\}\)) or certifies infeasibility. Equivalently (Theorem 5/12), for any finite abelian group \(G\) with \(|G|\le 4\) (more generally \(|G|-|R|\le 3\)), group-constrained TU feasibility \(Tx\le b,\ \gamma x\in R,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\) is solvable in strongly polynomial randomized time, and strictly 4-modular feasibility reduces to this via a strongly polynomial reduction (Lemma 8).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm (for each fixed constant \(\Delta\)) to solve general \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs \(\min\{c^\top x:Ax\le b, x\in\mathbb Z^n\}\) (optimization), assuming only bounded \(n\times n\) subdeterminants \(|\det(B)|\le \Delta\). This paper does not resolve that: it addresses a narrower subclass (strictly \(\Delta\)-modular, where full-size minors are restricted to \(\{-\Delta,0,\Delta\}\)) and only the feasibility problem (not optimization). It extends prior prime-modulus techniques by introducing group-constrained TU feasibility (GCTUF) and new decomposition/averaging arguments that avoid prime-number tools, yielding the first non-prime progress (\(\Delta=4\)). These techniques are relevant as strictly \(\Delta\)-modular feasibility is a key ingredient in known approaches to bounded-subdeterminant IPs, but the result falls short of solving general \(\Delta\)-modular optimization for arbitrary fixed \(\Delta\).

sources.title: Improving the Cook et al. proximity bound given integral valued constraints
sources.theorem_or_result: Improves the classical Cook–Gerards–Schrijver–Tardos proximity bound for ILPs with integral right-hand side: for integral full-column-rank \(A\in\mathbb Z^{m\times n}\), \(b\in\mathbb Z^m\), and an optimal LP vertex \(x^*\) of \(\max\{c^\top x:Ax\le b\}\), if an optimal integer solution exists then there is an optimal integer solution \(z^*\) with \(\|x^*-z^*\|_\infty < \tfrac n2\,\Delta_{n-1}(A)\). In the strictly \(\Delta\)-modular case \(A=TB\) (\(T\) TU, \(|\det B|=\Delta\)), they show \(\|x^*-z^*\|_\infty \le \max\{\Delta_{n-1}(A),\Delta_n(A)\}-1\), and provide near-matching lower bounds.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm for optimizing ILPs when \(A\) is \(\Delta\)-modular for fixed \(\Delta\). This paper does not give such an algorithm. Instead it strengthens proximity bounds (distance between an optimal LP vertex and an optimal integer solution) under the additional assumption that \(b\) is integral, with bounds expressed in terms of subdeterminants (notably \(\Delta_{n-1}(A)\)). Such proximity results are a standard ingredient for algorithm design (e.g., enumeration around an LP optimum, augmentation, or dynamic programming), and are particularly relevant in bounded-subdeterminant settings; however, the bound here still scales with \(n\) in general (\(\tfrac n2\Delta_{n-1}(A)\)), so it does not by itself yield a polynomial-time algorithm for fixed \(\Delta\) independent of dimension. The strictly \(\Delta\)-modular subclass result removes the \(n\) factor, but only for that special structure \(A=TB\), not for all \(\Delta\)-modular matrices.

sources.title: Proximity and flatness bounds for linear integer optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: For integral right-hand side \(b\in\mathbb Z^m\), they prove improved proximity/flatness bounds in terms of subdeterminants. In particular, if \(x^*\) is an optimal LP vertex of \(\max\{c^\top x:Ax\le b\}\) and the IP is feasible, then there exists an optimal integer solution \(z^*\) with \(\|x^*-z^*\|_\infty < \frac{4n+2}{9}\,\Delta_{n-1}(A)\) (Theorem 2). More generally, for any \(\alpha\in\mathbb Z^n\setminus\{0\}\), \(|\alpha^\top(x^*-z^*)|<\frac{4n+2}{9}\,\Delta_\alpha(A)\) where \(\Delta_\alpha(A)=\max\{|\det(\alpha^\top;B)|:B\text{ is an }(n-1)\times n\text{ submatrix of }A\}\) (Theorem 5). They also show a facet-direction flatness bound: if \(P(A,b)\) is full-dimensional lattice-free with facet-defining rows, then some row \(a\) of \(A\) satisfies \(w_a(P(A,b))<\frac{4n+2}{9}\,\Delta_n(A)-1\) (Theorem 4). In the special case that all \(n\times n\) minors lie in \(\{0,\pm k,\pm 2k\}\), they obtain dimension-independent bounds (Theorem 6).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm to solve ILPs when \(A\) is \(\Delta\)-modular (bounded \(n\times n\) subdeterminants) for fixed constant \(\Delta\). This paper does not give such an algorithm. Instead it develops structural bounds (proximity between LP and IP optima; flatness/lattice width bounds) in terms of \(\Delta_{n-1}(A)\) and \(\Delta_n(A)\) under the crucial assumption \(b\in\mathbb Z^m\). Such bounds are often key ingredients in algorithm design (e.g., bounding search neighborhoods around an LP optimum, or bounding widths used in Lenstra-type branching). Theorem 6 treats a restricted bounded-minor regime (minors in \(\{0,\pm k,\pm 2k\}\), including strictly \(\Delta\)-modular matrices \(A=TB\) with TU \(T\)), giving stronger, dimension-independent proximity/flatness bounds; this is closer in spirit to the bounded-subdeterminant algorithmic agenda but still falls short of solving general \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs.
